caption.text:
A train with 115 passengers was swallowed by the sea. The town never came back.

Dhanushkodi, at the southeastern tip of Pamban Island in Tamil Nadu, was a thriving port town. It had a railway station, a hospital, a higher secondary school, a post office, customs offices, and regular ferry service to Sri Lanka just 31 km away. The Geological Survey of India documented that the southern part of the township had already sunk by 5 metres due to tectonic shifts before December 1964.

Then the Rameswaram cyclone hit. Wind speeds touched 280 km/h. Over 1,800 people died in one night. The entire town was marooned. A train with all its passengers was washed to the bottom of the ocean. The Government of Madras declared Dhanushkodi a ghost town — officially unfit for human habitation.

The documented fact: the town was destroyed by natural forces. The gap? It was never rebuilt. Not a single structure was restored. The government wrote it off entirely. In 2004, just before the Indian Ocean tsunami, the sea receded 500 metres and exposed the submerged town — as if the ocean was giving it back for a moment. Then it took it away again.

Why does one destroyed town get rebuilt, and another get erased from memory?

text.line_2: A train swallowed by the sea.
